ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general black tea beverage class; no specific node for commercially flavoured RTD black tea. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKM covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific code for flavoured RTD black tea. Closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT code identified for commercially flavoured packaged black tea beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve bottle or can of packaged flavoured black tea is approximately 250 ml, equivalent to 250 g, providing about 50 kcal and 12.5 g of sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured black tea (packaged) — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for flavoured black tea (packaged), cr_id D09020024. All core macros and secondary nutrients are as reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sugar-sweetened beverages and tea-based drinks — analogous estimate based on sucrose and sweetened beverage GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for commercially flavoured packaged black tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the GI of sucrose (~65) and diluted sugar-sweetened beverages, adjusted for the low carbohydrate concentration (5.09 g/100 ml). Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (55 × 12.7 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.0. This is a conservative analogous estimate; actual GI may vary by formulation. GI of 55 is considered low according to University of Sydney GI Database thresholds (GI < 55 is low, 55-69 is medium, > 70 is high).

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) with 5.01 g sugar/100 ml; sodium is low at 9 mg/100 ml
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium content is very low (9 mg/100 ml), this beverage qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under AHA criteria; regular SSB consumption is independently associated with elevated blood pressure via insulin-mediated sodium retention, and individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid SSBs in favour of unsweetened black tea or water.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: SSB classification: 5.01 g free sugar/100 ml with zero dietary fibre, promoting rapid glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: This flavoured packaged black tea is a sugar-sweetened beverage with 5.01 g free sugar per 100 ml and zero dietary fibre, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this product and choose unsweetened black tea, which provides polyphenols without glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: SSB with added sugar; zero saturated fat and very low sodium are favourable, but free sugar intake is a CVD risk factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This beverage contains no saturated fat or cholesterol and has very low sodium (9 mg/100 ml), which are favourable for cardiovascular health; however, its free sugar content (5.01 g/100 ml) classifies it as an SSB, and habitual SSB consumption is associated with increased triglycerides, visceral adiposity, and cardiovascular risk — individuals with CVD should limit intake and prefer unsweetened tea.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar with zero anti-inflammatory nutrients; no omega-3, polyphenols, or fibre benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This packaged flavoured black tea contains added sugar and zero dietary fibre, with no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrient contribution; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose plain unsweetened black tea to obtain theaflavin polyphenols without the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured black tea (packaged, ready-to-drink) is a commercially manufactured sugar-sweetened beverage consisting of water, black tea extract, added sugar, and artificial or natural flavouring. It is sold in sealed bottles or cans and requires no preparation. The taste is mildly sweet with a light tea character; texture is thin and watery, similar to diluted sweetened tea. Per 100 ml it provides 20 kcal, 5.09 g carbohydrate (of which 5.01 g is free sugar), negligible protein and fat, and 9 mg sodium. Dietary fibre is absent. Clinically, this product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under both ADA and AHA guidelines due to its free sugar content and zero fibre. SSBs are associated with rapid postprandial glucose elevation, elevated triglycerides, and increased cardiovascular and hypertension risk. It is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease as a regular beverage. Unsweetened black tea is a preferable alternative, providing theaflavin and thearubigin polyphenols with negligible caloric impact. This product is widely available in Singapore and across Southeast Asia as a convenience beverage.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink flavoured black tea?
How much sugar is in bottled black tea?

Is flavoured black tea a sugar-sweetened beverage?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09020024) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from the approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate documented with rationale.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero: this is a water-based tea beverage with no protein-contributing ingredients. fat_g is naturally zero: this product contains no fat-contributing ingredients by formulation; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo — this is a filtered liquid beverage with no fibre. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific packaged flavoured black tea product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for sucrose and diluted sugar-sweetened beverages; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in approved sources. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish requiring mandatory local-language names; it is a generic commercially produced beverage and local_names fields are appropriately null. GI of 55 is considered low according to University of Sydney GI Database thresholds (GI < 55 is low, 55-69 is medium, > 70 is high).
